coredata.dc:description: The propensity of carbon dots (CDs) to undergo significant self-quenching in the solid state, whether due to excessive resonance energy transfer or direct molecule collision, has significantly limited their application in light-emitting diodes (LEDs). However, incorporating electron-deficient boron atoms, guanidine functional groups, and boron hydroxyl groups proves advantageous in forming a hydrogen bond structure network. This network effectively suppresses the self-quenching phenomenon observed in solid CDs, thereby enhancing their potential for use in LEDs. Therefore, on this basis, a series of multicolor tunable solid-state fluorescent CDs were obtained, and their formation mechanism was discussed. The structure and morphology of the prepared series of multicolor tunable CDs were also characterized. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of B-CD, Y-CD, and R-CD is 34.3 %, 23.7 %, and 20.5 %, respectively. The prepared series of CDs were used to successfully package the red, blue, and yellow monochromatic LEDs. By adjusting the ratio of CDs of different colors, a single matrix white-LEDs (w-LEDs), two primary colors w-LEDs, and the full-carbon-based w-LED of three primary colors are also obtained. The correlated color temperature of the above three w-LEDs is 7277, 3957, and 5040 K, the Color Rendering Index is 88, 91, and 92, and color coordinates are (0.302, 0.319), (0.376, 0.355), and (0.344, 0.354), respectively. It is demonstrated that CDs are a promising material in the solid-state lighting field.

head.ce:title: Gram-scale synthesis and optical properties of novel tunable solid-state fluorescent carbon dots with self-quenching-resistance for full-carbon-based w-LEDs

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The propensity of carbon dots (CDs) to undergo significant self-quenching in the solid state, whether due to excessive resonance energy transfer or direct molecule collision, has significantly limited their application in light-emitting diodes (LEDs). However, incorporating electron-deficient boron atoms, guanidine functional groups, and boron hydroxyl groups proves advantageous in forming a hydrogen bond structure network. This network effectively suppresses the self-quenching phenomenon observed in solid CDs, thereby enhancing their potential for use in LEDs. Therefore, on this basis, a series of multicolor tunable solid-state fluorescent CDs were obtained, and their formation mechanism was discussed. The structure and morphology of the prepared series of multicolor tunable CDs were also characterized. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of B-CD, Y-CD, and R-CD is 34.3 %, 23.7 %, and 20.5 %, respectively. The prepared series of CDs were used to successfully package the red, blue, and yellow monochromatic LEDs. By adjusting the ratio of CDs of different colors, a single matrix white-LEDs (w-LEDs), two primary colors w-LEDs, and the full-carbon-based w-LED of three primary colors are also obtained. The correlated color temperature of the above three w-LEDs is 7277, 3957, and 5040 K, the Color Rendering Index is 88, 91, and 92, and color coordinates are (0.302, 0.319), (0.376, 0.355), and (0.344, 0.354), respectively. It is demonstrated that CDs are a promising material in the solid-state lighting field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, have garnered significant attention and swiftly evolved into an outstanding fluorescent nanomaterial since their initial discovery in 2004 [1]. In contrast to conventional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, CDs not only inherit the attributes of carbon-based materials, such as low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility but also possess remarkable fluorescence tunability and exceptional water solubility [2–16]. They find wide-ranging applications in analytical detection, biological imaging, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), luminescent films, and more [2,16–26]. The technology used in producing CDs is environmentally friendly, cost-effective, highly flexible, scalable, thermally stable, and mechanically robust. It is particularly well-suited for solid-state fluorescent (SSF) systems, plant growth LEDs, and other applications [27–38]. Nonetheless, the fluorescence of CDs is often diminished or even completely extinguished when they aggregate, a phenomenon referred to as self-quenching in the solid state (SQS). Although the exact quenching mechanism remains inconclusive, it is widely believed that fluorescence quenching results from resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct π-π interactions among CDs [39,40]. It is also believed that there is a large overlap between the emission and absorption of CDs, which leads to fluorescence quenching due to the strong self-absorption effect in the solid state. Consequently, addressing SQS in CDs to achieve solid-state fluorescence has emerged as a prominent and active area of research [41–43].

White LEDs (w-LEDs) are progressively displacing traditional SSF lighting owing to their rapid response, energy efficiency, environmental friendliness, and high performance. Nevertheless, the current optical conversion materials for w-LEDs primarily rely on inorganic fluorescent powders and quantum dots, characterized by a complex preparation process and the use of expensive rare earth elements and heavy metals in their raw materials, thereby posing a threat to both resources and the environment [44–46]. In several studies, CDs have been explored as a novel light conversion material in LEDs. However, their widespread application has been hindered by SQS [19,47]. Zhou et al. introduced a method for producing highly luminescent solid-state CDs through space-constrained vacuum heating synthesis [48]. Meanwhile, Wan et al. synthesized hydrophilic yellow CDs with aggregation-induced emission characteristics using a microwave-assisted approach. Nevertheless, achieving convenient and robust preparation of solid-state luminescence without the need for a substrate remains a formidable task [49]. Despite some advances in SSF CDs, the development of multicolor tunable self-quenching-resistant CDs remains a challenge [50]. The most widely used way to produce w-LEDs is by combining a blue chip with a yellow phosphor [51]. By adjusting the color and amount of phosphor, w-LEDs with the different correlated color temperature (CCT) can be obtained. However, the absence of red components in their emission spectra leads to high CCT and low R a. [52].

There are two primary methods for CDs preparation: top-down and bottom-up. The bottom-up approach stands out due to its versatility in precursor selection and simplicity of preparation. Additionally, this method facilitates the easier introduction of heteroatoms during synthesis, enabling finer control over the emission wavelength of CDs and significantly enhancing luminous efficiency. This broadens the scope of applications for CDs. As early as 2010, Zhang et al. pioneered the use of the hydrothermal method, heating ascorbic acid to synthesize CDs. However, their quantum yield was only 6.79 % [53]. Subsequently, Yuan et al. employed phenylenediamine hydrothermal synthesis to generate multicolor fluorescent emission CDs, achieving quantum yields of 4.8 %, 10.4 %, and 20.6 % for blue, green, and red CDs, respectively [54]. Compared to the top-down method, the bottom-up approach offers superior solutions to the challenges of low quantum yield and limited color range. This advancement significantly expands the potential applications of CDs.

In this work, tartaric acid (TA) as a carbon source, arginine (Arg), and H3BO3 as nitrogen and boron doping to construct a kind of SSF CDs with self-quenching resistance. By modulating the reaction temperature, a range of adjustable multicolor SSF CDs were synthesized. Red-CDs (R-CDs) and yellow-CDs (Y-CDs) were combined to compensate for the absence of red components, resulting in the successful assembly of w-LEDs. Furthermore, a combination of blue-CDs (B-CDs), R-CDs, and Y-CDs was employed to create a full carbon-based w-LED.
TA, Arg, H3BO3, methanol, acetone, anhydrous ethanol, and UV curing packaging glue were produced by Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. None of the chemicals had been further purified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of a series of multicolor tunable CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of B-CDs: A one-step hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize water-soluble B-CDs. In a 100 mL beaker, 1.3810 g of TA (9.2 mmoL), 0.8000 g of Arg (4.6 mmoL), and 0.8000 g of H3BO3 (12.9 mmoL) were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water and stirred at room temperature for 10 min. This resulted in the formation of a uniform and transparent solution. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180℃ for 10 h. After cooling the reactor to room temperature, the large particles were filtered using a 0.22 μm disposable filter membrane to obtain a yellowish-clear liquid. The liquid was subsequently precipitated with acetone, washed three times with a methanol/acetone mixed solvent, and dried overnight under vacuum at 40℃. These steps yielded 1.1330 g of white B-CDs powders with a yield of 82 %.

Preparation of Y-CDs: The synthesis procedure for Y-CDs is similar to that of B-CDs, with the only difference being the reaction temperature, which is set at 200℃. Following the same steps, 0.9370 g of light-yellow Y-CDs powder was obtained, resulting in a yield of 68 %.

Preparation of R-CDs: The preparation of R-CDs involves using the same raw materials as B-CDs, but with a modified reaction temperature of 220℃. This leads to the formation of a brown transparent solution. After vacuum filtration using a 0.22 μm filter membrane, dialysis was performed for 3 days using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da. This process helps remove any unreacted raw materials and impurities, with the water being changed every 4 h during dialysis. Finally, 1.0390 g of R-CDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying, resulting in a yield of 75 %.

Three types of monochrome LEDs were fabricated using the same processes. To elaborate, in the case of the blue LED, a small amount of B-CDs (0.5 mg) dispersed in the silicone binder. Then, the mixtures were dropped onto the n-UV LED chip (395 nm) and solidified under an n-UV lamp for 3 min. When driving the fabricated LEDs at 350 mA and 3 V voltages, the electroluminescent (EL) spectra, CCT, R a, and CIE chromaticity coordinates of the prepared w-LED were measured using USB 4000 (Ocean Optics, USA).

Furthermore, three varieties of w-LEDs were manufactured, comprising the single matrix w-LED (utilizing a 465 nm chip and Y-CDs), the two primary colors w-LED (employing a 465 nm chip in combination with Y-CDs (0.6 mg) and R-CDs (0.3 mg)), and the full-carbon-based w-LED (featuring a 395 nm chip along with Y-CDs (0.5 mg), R-CDs (0.3 mg), and B-CDs (0.3 mg)). The fabrication method for the w-LEDs was consistent with that of the blue LED. It is noteworthy that the operational voltage for all the LEDs stands at 3.6 V.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected using Tecnai G2 Spirit Bio (FEI). The Malvern MS2000 laser particle size analyzer was utilized to measure the size distribution. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiation ranging from 10° to 70° was tested by a German X-ray diffractometer (D2 PHASER, Bruker). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Vetex70, Bruker) was used to study the functional groups within 4000–400 cm−1 through the KBr pellet method. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was measured by a Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD photoelectron spectrometer. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum, photoluminescence excitation, decay lifetimes, and photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of CDs were measured by a spectrometer (FLS-980, Edinburgh, Britain). To document the fluorescence spectra across various operational temperatures, a fluorescence spectrometer F4600 by Hitachi, manufactured in Japan, was employed in conjunction with a temperature-regulated accessory. The parameters of fabricated LEDs (350 mA current and 3 V voltages) were analyzed by a fiber optic spectrometer (USB 4000, Ocean Optics, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of tunable multicolor SSF CDs
ce:section.ce:para: By carefully controlling the reaction temperature, multicolor tunable SSF CDs were synthesized. As illustrated in Fig. 1(a), three distinct types of CDs were obtained at different temperatures: 180℃, 200℃, and 220℃. With an increase in reaction temperature, the color of the CDs powders gradually darkened under daylight, indicating a higher degree of carbonization. When exposed to 365 nm UV light, three types of CDs emitted light in different colors, ranging from bright blue to yellow to red. To examine the effect of hydrothermal temperatures on the CDs spectra, further research was carried out. Fig. 1(b) displays the normalized PL emission spectra of the B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs samples at their respective optimal excitation wavelengths, revealing a noticeable redshift phenomenon. Under 344 nm excitation, B-CDs exhibited an emission peak at 411 nm. Under 465 nm excitation, Y-CDs displayed an emission peak at 542 nm. Under 585 nm excitation, Y-CDs showed an emission peak at 660 nm. The full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission peaks for B-, Y-, and R-CDs were calculated as 82 nm, 117 nm, and 183 nm, respectively. The higher reaction temperature facilitated the formation of CDs with a greater degree of graphitization, which can account for the observed redshift phenomenon [55,56]. The normalized PL emission spectra of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs at 395 nm excitation are shown in Fig. 1(c). Compared with Fig. 1(b), the emission peaks of B-CDs and Y-CDs are redshifted, while the position of R-CDs emission peaks does not change, which is described in detail in Fig. 5. In Fig. 1(d), the color coordinates of B-, Y-, and R-CDs are depicted at their respective optimal excitation wavelengths, with each type located in the blue, yellow, and red regions, respectively. These results demonstrate the successful preparation of continuously multicolor tunable SSF CDs by precisely controlling the reaction temperature.

In Fig. 2(a–c), the TEM images reveal that all CDs exhibit a spherical structure with excellent monodispersity. To estimate particle size, the number of B-CDs, Y-CDs, R-CDs particles was 144, 72 116, respectively. The particle sizes of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs were determined to be 6.39(3) nm, 14.36(6) nm, and 23.61(4) nm, respectively. As the hydrothermal reaction temperature increases, the carbonization degree also increases, resulting in larger particle sizes and a consequent redshift in the emitted light of the CDs. This phenomenon can be attributed to the quantum size effect [57].

Fig. 3(a) presents the XRD patterns of CDs synthesized at different temperatures. The three types of SSF CDs exhibit a broad diffraction peak near 2θ = 21°, indicating the presence of amorphous carbon, as evidenced by the typical (002) diffraction peak [58]. Notably, R-CDs synthesized at 220℃ exhibit an additional diffraction peak at 2θ = 27°, suggesting the emergence of a graphene structure [59]. Furthermore, it is observed that the peak shape of the CDs (at 2θ = 21°) becomes broader and shifts towards the left with increasing reaction temperature. This shift may be attributed to the presence of functional groups at the edge of the CDs, which increases the layer spacing and causes the diffraction peak to shift. The presence of abundant surface groups contributes to the amorphous structure of the surface of CDs, resulting in a broader peak pattern [60,61]. To gain a clearer understanding of the surface groups present in the CDs, FT-IR analysis was conducted to investigate their composition. As depicted in Fig. 3(b), the absorption bands observed at 3460 cm−1 to 2926 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of ν(O-H) and ν(N-H), indicating the presence of hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of the CDs. These functional groups contribute to enhanced hydrophilicity and stability. Additionally, the stretching vibrations of CO (1743 cm−1), CN (1669 cm−1), C−B (1088 cm−1), B−O (1332 cm−1), and the bending vibrations of B−O−H (1173 cm−1) are observed in all three CDs samples. These findings further confirm the incorporation of nitrogen and boron in the synthesis of CDs [62,63]. Notably, a new absorption peak at 1394 cm−1 is observed in R-CDs, which can be attributed to the stretching vibration of CC. This suggests an increase in the degree of carbonization and the formation of a conjugated structure following the temperature rise [64,65].

To further elucidate the influence of surface functional groups on the fluorescence mechanism of the CDs, the XPS analysis was performed on B-, Y-, and R-CDs. As shown in Fig. 4(a–c), the XPS spectra exhibit four characteristic peaks at 190.8, 284.6, 398.9, and 530.8 eV, corresponding to B 1 s, C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. This confirms the successful preparation of nitrogen and boron-doped multicolor SSF CDs. Table 1 summarizes the changes in the content of each element (C, N, O, and B) resulting from temperature variations. The carbon content increases significantly with the rise in reaction temperature. Moreover, the calculated oxygen-to-carbon relative atomic ratio (O/C) gradually decreases in B-CDs (54.23 %), Y-CDs (37.68 %), and R-CDs (29.25 %), indicating a progressive increase in dehydration. As depicted in Fig. 4(d-f), the high-resolution C 1 s spectra reveal that nitrogen and boron-doped CDs exhibit four distinct carbon signals: C−B, CC/C−C, C−N/C−O, and COOH. The relative content values of these carbon signals, calculated based on their peak areas, are presented in Table 2. It is evident that the relative content of C-B and CN gradually decreases in the sequence of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs, indicating a reduction in the surface functional groups of the CDs. The increased relative content of CC/C−C suggests an increase of the sp2 hybridization carbon, leading to greater delocalization of π electrons. This, in turn, reduces the band gap and causes the emitted light from the CDs to shift towards longer wavelengths, resulting in a redshift [66]. Furthermore, the relative content of carboxyl groups increases with the redshift in emission [67]. Fig. 4(g-i) displays the high-resolution O 1 s spectra, revealing two oxygen signals, CO and C−OH/C−O−C, present in B-, Y-, and R-CDs. The content of the CO peak increases, while the content of the C−OH/C−O−C peak decreases, indicating a gradual increase in the degree of dehydration and carbonization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of multicolor SSF CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 5(a, b), B-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent luminescence behavior like previously reported CDs [68–70]. When the excitation wavelength is increased from 324 nm to 394 nm, the emission of B-CDs undergoes a redshift from 403 nm to 459 nm. The strongest emission of B-CDs occurs at 411 nm under 344 nm excitation. However, as the excitation wavelength exceeds 344 nm, the emission of B-CDs continues to redshift, albeit with a gradual decrease in luminescence intensity. Fig. 5(c, d) displays the excitation-emission curves and PL emission spectra of Y-CDs under different excitation wavelengths, which exhibit a similar excitation-dependent behavior. With an increase in excitation wavelength from 367 nm to 527 nm, the emission of Y-CDs redshifts from 542 nm to 564 nm. Notably, when the excitation wavelength is below 487 nm, the emission wavelength remains unchanged. In Fig. 5(e, f), the intrinsic luminescence of R-CDs is presented. The emission wavelength of R-CDs remains approximately 660 nm across the excitation wavelength range of 365 nm to 635 nm, indicating an excitation-independent characteristic [63]. The fluorescence emission of CDs with smaller particle sizes is determined by surface energy states, such as functional group variations, while in CDs with larger particle sizes, the fluorescence emission is governed by the size of the carbon skeleton. Consequently, CDs with a uniform particle size distribution do not exhibit excitation-dependent properties [71]. Due to significant carbonization, the particle size of Y-CDs increases, resulting in excitation dependence only becoming evident when there is a substantial increase in the excitation wavelength.

The emission spectra of B-, Y-, and R-CDs were evaluated under varying temperatures, as depicted in Fig. 6. From Fig. 6(a–c), it is evident that the emission peak intensity of B-, Y-, and R-CDs gradually decreases from 300 K to 480 K. This decline can be attributed to the significant increase in non-radiative relaxation rates with rising temperature, resulting in reduced fluorescence emission intensity [72]. Fig. 6(d–f) illustrates the normalized fluorescence intensity plots of B-, Y-, and R-CDs with temperature variations, respectively. It is observed that T0.5 exceeds 480 K, indicating that the temperature at which the emission intensity reaches 50 % of its initial value signifies good thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of self-quenching-resistance and luminescence redshift of multicolor SSF CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7(a) illustrates the photographs of B-CDs under daylight and UV light. From left to right, the images display undoped B-CDs, boron-doped B-CDs, nitrogen-doped B-CDs, and nitrogen, boron-codoped B-CDs. It was observed that undoped B-CDs exhibit no self-quenching resistance and display minimal fluorescence under UV light. The fluorescence of CDs doped with either nitrogen or boron is stronger compared to undoped CDs when exposed to UV light. The co-doping of nitrogen and boron demonstrates a synergistic effect, further enhancing the self-quenching resistance and fluorescence performance of CDs. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the fluorescence intensity of nitrogen, boron-codoped B-CDs is the highest. Therefore, the selection of suitable nitrogen and boron sources is crucial for synthesizing CDs with self-quenching resistance. The SSF CDs were realized by inhibiting the charge transfer of CDs via introducing an electron-deficient boron atom of H3BO3. In addition, hydrogen bonds were constructed by B–OH functional groups of H3BO3 and the guanidine group of Arg, which maintained CDs in a dispersed state akin to that in solution [73,74]. The synergistic effect between these two mechanisms further enhances the self-quenching resistance and fluorescence performance of CDs. Consequently, only CDs modified with Arg and H3BO3 possess the property of self-quenching resistance.

Generally, the PL mechanisms of the three CDs can be explained by the synergistic interaction of carbon core and surface states. By controlling the reaction temperature, the graphitization and surface functionalization of CDs can be manipulated, thereby altering the HOMO and LUMO energy level structure of CDs and achieving regulation of solid-state fluorescence. This concept is illustrated in Fig. 7(c). Increasing the sp2 carbon and carboxyl group content of CDs can induce emission redshift. Due to the conjugation of the lone pair electrons of the amide group on the CDs surface with the carbon core, the emission redshift is caused [19]. Meanwhile, it is observed that the excitation and emission spectra of CDs partially overlap, and there is absorption overlap between CDs emitting at shorter and longer wavelengths, leading to RET between CDs and resulting in a redshift in luminescence. As the reaction temperature increases, CDs tend to aggregate, leading to an increase in particle size. When the distance between CDs is smaller than the critical distance (R 0) for RET to occur, fluorescence energy transfer from the donor to the acceptor is observed, causing a redshift in the aggregated solid state, as depicted in Fig. 7(d).

To confirm the occurrence of the RET process, the fluorescence decay time of three SSF CDs was measured, as shown in Fig. 8. The fitted data ( Table 3), the τ
AV -values of B-, Y-, and R-CDs were 7.226(1) ns, 4.654(3) ns, and 2.141(2) ns. The gradual decrease in the decay time value is due to the increase in non-radiative transitions. The lifetimes of B-CDs and Y-CDs are marginally longer compared to CDs prepared by Chen et al. using phenyl diamonium as the carbon source, which exhibited a lifetime of 4.1 ns [75]. Similarly, they exceed the lifetimes of CDs synthesized by Liu et al. with self-quenching resistance, which demonstrated a lifetime of 4 ns [76]. This observation provides compelling evidence for the luminous redshift resulting from RET among CDs. The absolute PLQY of B-CDs, Y-CDs, and R-CDs under optimal excitation was further characterized using an integrating sphere. The solid-state PLQY of B-CDs remained at 34.3 %, surpassing that of Y-CDs at 23.7 % and R-CDs at 20.5 %.

Fig. 9(a-c) displays the electroluminescence (EL) spectra of monochrome LED devices packaged with B-, Y-, and R-CDs, representing blue, yellow, and red emission, respectively. The CIE color coordinates of the corresponding monochrome LEDs are in the blue, yellow, and red regions, as shown in Fig. 9(d). The schematic of a fully packaged LED device and the luminescent image of the fabricated LED device are shown in Fig. 9(e, f). These results demonstrate the feasibility of utilizing the three CDs to fabricate monochrome LED devices.

Fig. 10(a–e) illustrates the electroluminescence (EL) spectra and accompanying photographs of w-LED devices that have been packaged with GaN blue chips (465 nm), with varying concentrations of Y-CDs. Within the EL spectrum, the peak at 465 nm signifies the emission originating from the blue chip, while the broader emission peak at 550 nm emanates from the Y-CDs. Through adjustments in the Y-CDs concentration, it becomes possible to tune the ratio of these two peaks, thereby achieving the creation of a single matrix w-LED with distinct CCT, R a, and CIE coordinates. Analyzing Fig. 10(f), the CIE color coordinate diagram, it becomes evident that the CIE positions of the w-LEDs progressively shift from the blue region, traversing the white region, and eventually landing within the yellow region. Table 4 presents the detailed encapsulation of these five w-LEDs and their pertinent CIE, CCT, and R a parameters. Notably, w-LED-3 stands out with a color coordinate of (0.302, 0.319), a R a of 88, and a CCT of 7277 K—metrics that closely approximate the international standard for white light (0.333, 0.333), signifying a cold white light source. At 3 V and 350 mA, the luminance and efficiency of w-LED-3 are 6972 cd m−2 and 3.89 lm W−1, respectively. These results conclusively demonstrate that the synthesized Y-CDs are exceptionally well-suited for applications in outdoor lighting and various scenarios requiring cold white illumination.

In the work above, w-LED-3 to w-LED-5 exhibit high CCT and low R a due to the absence of red components. To address this, the R-CDs and Y-CDs were combined in varying proportions to compensate for the lack of red components, resulting in the development of w-LED-6 to w-LED-10. Fig. 11(a–e) displays the EL spectra of w-LED-6 to w-LED-10, with the emission peak at 465 nm representing the blue chip, while the broad emission peak ranging from 550 to 700 nm is attributed to the combined contributions of Y-CDs and R-CDs. This approach successfully yields w-LEDs with warm white light, and by adjusting the proportion of red components, both CCT and R a can be fine-tuned. Furthermore, Fig. 11(f) demonstrates the transition of the CIE color coordinates corresponding to different w-LEDs from the cold white light region to the warm white light region along the color temperature curve. Notably, w-LED-6 possesses a color coordinate of (0.376, 0.355), a CCT of 3957 K, and an impressive R a of 91, exemplifying the achievement of a warm w-LED with high color rendering index (CRI). At 3 V and 350 mA, the luminance and efficiency of w-LED-6 are 7209 cd m−2 and 3.56 lm W−1, respectively. Table 5 provides the relevant parameters for the w-LEDs-6–10. The results confirm that the developed SSF CDs are well-suited for indoor healthy lighting applications and other scenarios requiring warm white light.

Finally, a full-carbon-based warm w-LED was successfully synthesized utilizing B-, Y-, and R-CDs and UV chip (395 nm). The corresponding EL spectra and the LED photograph are displayed in Fig. 12. Due to the broad emission profile of CDs, a distinct demarcation between the emissions of the three types of CDs was not observed. Consequently, individual fits were applied to each CDs emission, as illustrated in Fig. 12. The resulting CIE color coordinates for the w-LED were determined to be (0.344, 0.354), closely aligning with the international standard for white light (0.333, 0.333). This achievement was accompanied by a CCT of 5040 K and a R a of 92. At 3 V and 350 mA, the luminance and efficiency of the full-carbon-based warm w-LED are 7580 cd m−2 and 3.59 lm W−1, respectively. Therefore, the full-carbon-based w-LED can emit a full spectrum encompassing the entire visible region, thereby indicating its considerable potential for a wide array of applications, particularly in display lighting.

A simple and novel gram-scale preparation method of nitrogen, boron-codoped tunable SSF CDs (B-, Y-, R-CDs) with self-quenching resistance was presented. The synergistic effect of nitrogen and boron co-doping effectively mitigates the quenching phenomenon resulting from CDs aggregation. Moreover, it was observed that alterations in particle size and surface functional groups of CDs could be controlled by adjusting the hydrothermal reaction temperature. The PL mechanisms of the three CDs can be explained by the synergistic interaction of carbon core and surface states. As the reaction temperature increased, the CDs particle size enlarged, leading to a redshift in both absorption and emission spectra. Further analysis through FT-IR and XPS techniques revealed an augmented sp2 carbon and an increased carboxyl group content in CDs, contributing to the observed emission redshift. Additionally, overlapping absorption between short-wavelength emission CDs and long-wavelength emission CDs was identified, facilitating RET between CDs and consequently contributing to the emission redshift of CDs. The photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of B-CD, Y-CD, and R-CD is 34.3 %, 23.7 %, and 20.5 %, respectively. In an application context, Y-CDs and a blue light chip were employed to create a cool w-LED characterized by color coordinates of (0.302, 0.319), CCT = 7277 K, R a = 83. Through this R-CDs modification, the CCT and R a of the warm w-LED were adjusted, resulting in a warm light with a lower CCT of 3957 K and a higher R a of 91. The resulting color coordinate of the warm w-LED was found to be (0.376, 0.355), closely resembling the color coordinate of the international standard for white light. Lastly, the packaging of the full-carbon-based w-LED involved the utilization of three types of self-quenching resistance SSF CDs, with CIE chromaticity is (0.344, 0.357), CCT = 5040 K, and R a = 95. This aspect indicates the potential of the CDs prepared using the method to significantly contribute to advancements in the field of solid-state lighting.
